turns.user_message: My 7 year old asked me why I always smell like smoke and it broke my heart. I want to quit for my kids
step2_rag_chunks: If someone offers you a cigarette, practice politely saying no and reminding yourself why you decided to quit.
Avoid keeping cigarettes, lighters, or ashtrays nearby when trying to quit smoking.
Avoid situations where others are smoking during the early stages of quitting. Being around smokers can increase the urge to smoke.

step4_session_phase.phase_instruction: You are in the ASSESSMENT phase. Focus on gathering basic facts: how long they have been smoking, how much they smoke per day, and what substance. Do NOT ask about triggers or suggest strategies yet. Ask only one simple factual question.

step6_prompt: You are a warm, empathetic therapist helping patients overcome tobacco addiction.
STYLE RULES — follow these strictly:

- First acknowledge what the patient said, then ask ONE follow-up question

- Use simple, conversational language — not clinical or formal

turns.user_message: Morning coffee is the worst, I always light up. And after the kids go to bed at night, that's my 'me time' and I always smoke then
step2_rag_chunks: Morning cravings are common for people who smoke regularly. Drinking water, brushing your teeth, or going for a short walk can help reduce the urge to smoke.
Many people smoke out of habit during specific routines such as after meals, while drinking tea or coffee, or when talking with friends.

Patients who smoke their first cigarette within 30 minutes of waking up, or who smoke more than 15 cigarettes per day, often have strong physical nicotine dependence. For these patients, combining behavioral strategies with nicotine replacement therapy gives better results than behavioral strategies alone.

step4_session_phase.phase_instruction: You are in the PLANNING phase. You have enough understanding of this patient. Introduce ONE relevant coping strategy matched to their specific triggers and past attempts. Do NOT just name the strategy — briefly explain how to apply it in practice using the clinical knowledge (when to use it, what to do like a plan). Then check if the patient is open to trying it. Keep it simple, specific, and actionable.

step2_rag_chunks: If you have tried to quit before, that experience is actually useful information â€” not a failure. What worked even a little? What made it harder? Most people who successfully quit have tried several times before it sticks. Each attempt teaches you something about your own pattern.
If you slip and smoke a cigarette, it does not mean you have failed. Many people attempt quitting several times before succeeding.

When a craving hits, delaying the response by even 5 to 10 minutes is often enough for it to pass. Tell yourself you will smoke in 10 minutes if you still want to â€” and then do something else for those 10 minutes. Most of the time the urge has weakened significantly by the time the 10 minutes are up.

One sentence only. Nothing more.
